narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was carefully evaluated by our admissions model, which looks at patterns from thousands of past students to predict bar exam success. The model assessed your Year 1 and Year 3 school grade deciles, both at the lowest level of 1—far below the typical student's mid-range performance—along with your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, LSAT score of 27, and plans to attend full-time. It predicted a 96% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected; the school prioritizes admitting those likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, as being in the bottom of your class at that stage strongly signals challenges ahead compared to peers who climb higher. Your Year 1 decile also played a major role, reinforcing concerns since it too was at the very bottom, unlike the average applicant. Even your LSAT score, well below the dataset average, contributed noticeably by suggesting hurdles in the analytical skills needed for the bar. Less but still notable was your middle family income bracket, which didn't provide the same boost as higher ones often do for other students. Your undergraduate GPA edged the prediction slightly toward failure, as it trailed the typical 3.21 average, while your full-time status had a minimal push in that direction—though most applicants share this and fare better overall. In contrast to typical students, who often have stronger deciles, higher test scores, and GPAs that align with bar passers, your profile across these key areas stood out as riskier. This isn't a judgment on your potential or character, but a data-driven call to protect the school's outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and exploring part-time options if they fit better—then reapply when your profile better matches successful peers. We're rooting for your success in law, and targeted improvements can change the story.
